segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara and Elowen kept companionable silence for a long time, because both understood that speech could wake things that needed to sleep -- grief, doubt, the careful accounting of what each of them was losing -- and they needed rest more than they needed honesty.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen sat cross-legged, staff across her knees, eyes closed. The vine-ink along her forearms pulsed in counterpoint to the Hall's rhythm -- emerald and silver, a quiet conversation between the druid and the mountain that needed no interpreter.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara sat opposite, legs crossed at the ankle, sleeves drawn carefully over her fingertips with the practiced casualness of someone who'd turned concealment into habit. She watched the chalk mark nearest the Necromancy alcove the way you watch a door you expect bad news from.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: When she finally spoke, it was to the chalk mark.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Your threads are brighter."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen glanced down at the silver braided through her vine-ink.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Borrowed,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "I'll pay back the interest."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara tipped her head.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "It hurts when you try to ease him now."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Yes."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen's smile was almost tender, as if at a seedling growing in the wrong direction.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "So I won't try unless I must."

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "You'll be tempted,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara said.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen didn't deny it.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I will."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A pause. The lichen pulsed. Then Nyxara pulled one sleeve back, just far enough. The fingertips were black past the second knuckle now, the skin with the texture of something learning to be dead while the rest of her disagreed.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "How many can you lose?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara asked, nodding at Elowen's tattoos.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen was quiet. When she spoke, her voice had the steadiness of someone who'd done arithmetic she wished she hadn't.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Each mark is a connection to the Weave. To the circle that trained me. To the part of me that can hear what the forest hears."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She touched the faded sage where emerald used to be.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "When this one went quiet, I stopped hearing birdsong in my sleep. That sounds small."

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "It isn't,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara said, and meant it.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I have seventeen. I've dimmed three. When the count reaches nine -- half -- I won't feel the Weave at all. I'll be a woman with interesting skin and a walking stick."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara's mouth twitched -- not a smile, exactly, but the memory of where a smile would go.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "A woman with interesting skin and a walking stick is not nothing."

segments.speaker: elowen
segments.text: "No,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen agreed.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "But it's not a druid."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The silence settled between them. Nyxara looked at her own dark fingers -- flexed them once, methodically, the way you test a hinge.

segments.speaker: elowen
segments.text: "My patron isn't patient. She isn't kind. She is reliable, which is more than I can say for most contracts I've honored."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The clinical voice of a woman discussing weather that might kill her.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "At this rate, the hand goes by winter. The arm by spring."

segments.speaker: elowen
segments.text: "And then?"

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "And then I renegotiate, or I stop being useful, or she finds another carrier. Patrons are like landlords -- they don't care about the tenant, only whether the property appreciates."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara tucked the sleeve back with care.

segments.speaker: elowen
segments.text: "The question isn't how much you lose. It's what you do with what's left."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Elowen looked at the darkened fingers. Looked at Nyxara's face -- the mask there, and the exhaustion behind it.

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "My circle-mother told me something the day the circle broke. She said: Decide who you are when the Weave doesn't answer. I've been working on the answer for two years."

segments.speaker: elowen
segments.text: "And?"

segments.speaker: nyxara
segments.text: "I'll let you know."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The lichen pulsed. The Hall breathed. Two women losing themselves by inches sat in the dark and let the silence hold what words couldn't carry.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Fourth watch: Durgan alone.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik was meant to be with him -- Serana's assignment, careful and deliberate -- but Thornik had fallen asleep again sitting up, grand plans overridden by honest biology. Durgan didn't wake him. He was better alone for this. He needed to be alone for this.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stood with the chalk sigil's white geometry between him and the sleeping forms of six people he could name, and his shadow studied the distance.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: It began as a suggestion. The shadow stretched until it kissed the threshold line, then curled away like a cat from a hot stove. He felt the push -- the thing in him testing, calculating, measuring angles and distances with a cartographer's precision. He breathed. Held. Pressed it flat.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A vision slid across his awareness without knocking.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The central table. Caelin asleep on a bedroll, scale bare in the low light, the coal-glow steady as a sleeping heartbeat. Durgan's hand on a knife. Four steps. Kneel.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Angle of blade: shallow and long to avoid arteries, deep enough to free the hex-plate, twist at the notch where obsidian meets flesh --

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He jerked back into himself with a soundless gasp, hand already halfway to his belt. He closed his fist on empty air until pain brought him back into the body that had not moved.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The visions were getting worse. Not louder -- clearer. More detailed. More instructive. As if whatever lived in his shadow had given up on temptation and moved to education, deciding that if he wouldn't take the scale willingly, he should at least know the correct technique for when his will broke.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A hot line opened under his nose. He wiped the blood on the heel of his palm and watched it dry black in the lichen light.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Minutes between episodes now. Hours before. Days once.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stepped deliberately backward from the chalk and stared at the pillar carvings until the urge lost its edges -- the images of dwarves and dragons working together, the old alliances, the cooperation between things that should have been enemies.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stared at that until the knife in his mind became just a knife, and the four steps became just distance, and the angle of blade became just geometry.

segments.speaker: elowen
segments.text: "I will walk away before I can't stop it,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he told the stone under his breath, and the mountain, practical and ancient, declined to promise anything.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stood until false dawn turned the lichen grey. He did not sleep. He did not move. He held the door closed against something that pushed harder every day, and counted the cost in blood that dried black on his palm.
